sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Ankle swelling and difficulty breathing.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing ankle swelling that was not responsive to elevation and rest. She also reported difficulty breathing, requiring propping herself up in bed to facilitate breathing. She sought medical attention after experiencing difficulty talking and was transported by ambulance to the emergency department. She has never had an ambulance ride before. The patient reports a history of liking potato chips and salt and vinegar potato chips, and admits to eating them daily. She has been watching her diet but admits to some slips. She reports feeling better now but still experiences shortness of breath with exertion.
Findings
Vital signs reveal a blood pressure of 128 over 72 mmHg, a temperature of 98.7°F, a heart rate of 72 bpm, and respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute with an oxygen saturation of 96%. Examination reveals trace edema in the lower extremities. A two over six systolic ejection murmur is present. The echocardiogram demonstrates a preserved ejection fraction of 55% and abnormal diastolic filling with mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation. There is no jugular venous distention. The lungs are clear bilaterally.
Assessment
Congestive heart failure secondary to hypertension and mitral regurgitation.
Plan
medical_concepts: trace edema in the lower extremities

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: Continue the patient on bumex 2 milligrams once daily. Advise the patient to continue watching her diet and avoid salty foods. Encourage daily weight monitoring and call if she gains three pounds in two days. Order a renal artery ultrasound to evaluate for renal artery stenosis. Initiate a referral to a registered dietitian for dietary counseling. Order a blood pressure cuff for home monitoring and instruct the patient to keep a log of blood pressure readings and dietary intake. Follow up in three months.

medical_concepts: trace edema in the lower extremities
